title: Decode Ways
problem_statement: A message containing letters from A-Z is encoded to numbers using the mapping 'A'->"1", 'B'->"2", ..., 'Z'->"26". Given a digit string s, return the number of ways to decode it. The string may contain leading zeros, and any invalid encoding should be counted as zero.
intuition: At each index, count decodings by using either one digit or two digits if the pair forms a valid number between 10 and 26.

approaches.name: Recursive Brute Force
approaches.description: Try decoding one digit and two digits from the current position, and sum the valid paths.
approaches.why_not: Exponential because it explores both one-digit and two-digit options at each position without memoization.

approaches.name: Memoized Recursion (Top-Down DP)
approaches.description: Cache the number of decodings from each index to avoid re-evaluating the same suffix multiple times.
approaches.why_not: Still recursive, but memoization makes it linear time. Bottom-up removes recursion overhead.

approaches.name: Bottom-Up Dynamic Programming
approaches.description: Use a 1D dp array where dp[i] counts decodings for s[0..i-1], and update it based on one-digit and two-digit validity.
approaches.why_not: This is the standard optimal solution for decoding count.

interview_qa.q: How do zeros affect the decoding?
interview_qa.a: A zero cannot be decoded on its own. It must be paired with a preceding '1' or '2' to form 10 or 20.
dp[i] = dp[i-1] if single digit is valid + dp[i-2] if two-digit slice is valid.
It represents one way to decode an empty prefix and simplifies the recurrence for the first valid digit.
Any substring starting with '0' that is not part of '10' or '20' results in zero valid decodings.
